segments.s549-g17: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'); the phrase ما زنده اندر زمان توایم literally follows Subject (ما) + predicate phrase. اندر = 'in/within' is a poetic preposition placed before زمان. توایم is an archaic/poetic fused form: تو‑ + ایم (1st‑person plural copula), here giving the sense 'we are with/attached to you' and links back to ما as the subject.
segments.s549-g18: به هر کار نیکی is a prepositional phrase ('in/for every good deed') where به functions as 'in/with/regarding' in a poetic register; نیکی qualifies کار ('deed'). گمان is a noun meaning 'expectation/thought' and takes the following predicate توایم (again the poetic 'we are with you'), so syntactically the clause says 'our thought/expectation is of you' in relation to every good deed.
segments.s549-g19: خریدار is a noun meaning 'buyer' or 'one who would pay for'; it heads the noun phrase خریدار دیدار چهر ترا where دیدار (a noun) follows as the object ('the sight/meeting of') and چهر + ت + را is 'your face' with the enclitic ت marking the second person and را the object marker. Word order is noun (one who buys) + object phrase.
segments.s549-g20: همان here functions as 'the same/likewise' introducing parallel items. خوب‌گفتار is an adjectival compound 'good-spoken' (eloquent, kind in speech). و links it to مهر ترا ('your affection'), where ت is the 2nd‑person suffix and را again marks the object/thing being referred to; the clause lists what one would 'buy' (see previous segment).
segments.s549-g21: This line is two short clauses linked: تو ایمن بوی is an archaic optative/subjunctive wish ('may you be safe') where بوی is an old form of the verb 'be' (not the modern noun 'smell'); کز introduces the reason/clause 'that/from which' and connects to تو ما ایمنیم ('by you we are safe' or 'because of you we are secure'). ایمنیم is the present plural predicative 'we are safe'.
segments.s549-g22: مبادا ک(ه) introduces a prohibition or fearful wish ('may it not be / lest that ...'). پیمان تو is 'your covenant/pledge' (تو as possessive/second‑person marker), and بشکنیم is the verb in present/subjunctive plural 'we break' (here 'may we not break'), so the whole clause expresses a plea not to break the covenant.
segments.s549-g23: تو is the subject 'you'; بستی is the past/perfective form of بستن used here as 'you closed/blocked'; ره is the direct object 'way/road' placed after the verb in poetic order. بدسگالان ما is a noun phrase 'our ill‑fated/ill‑omened ones' where ما is the possessive 'our' after the noun (poetic placement).
segments.s549-g24: A simple enumerative prepositional list: ز + هند ('from India') then و, ز + چین ('from China'), و introduces همالان ما ('and our bearers/porters/companions'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; چین here denotes the country China (poetic geography).
segments.s549-g41: بماناد is an optative/archaic form meaning 'may remain' (a wish); it comes first for poetic emphasis. این شاه is the subject 'this king' placed after the verb, and با مهر و داد is a prepositional phrase 'with love and justice' modifying how he should remain.
segments.s549-g42: ندارد is a present negative verb 'does not have/possess' with the logical subject following (جهان = 'the world'). چون تو خسرو is a comparative/appositive phrase 'like you, Khosrow' (خسرو used as a proper name here), and به یاد is the adverbial phrase 'in memory' that completes what the world lacks.
segments.s549-g43: This clause states a provenance/possession: جهان یکسر 'the world entirely' + از 'is from' followed by رای و فر 'your counsel and (royal) glory'. The final تست here is an archaic/copular ending (classical poetic contraction of 'توست' / 'it is [yours]') placed at the end; it functions as the copula linking the phrase to the speaker's claim.
segments.s549-g44: خنک is a predicate adjective 'refreshing/pleasant'. آنک (classical form of آن‌که) is a relative pronoun 'he who/that which'. در سایه پر means 'full/in the shade' (در سایه = 'in the shade', پر = 'full [of]' or 'abundant'), and the final تست again functions as the archaic copula 'is'. Together: 'Refreshing is he who is abundantly in your shade.'
segments.s549-g45: همیشه 'always' opens the wish; سر تخت 'the head/top of the throne' is the locative/positional phrase, جای تو 'your place/seat' is the thing wished for, and باد is the optative particle 'may' (a fixed poetic wish form). Word order places the time adverb then the location, then the possessor, finishing with the optative.
segments.s549-g46: This is another optative clause: جهان 'the world' + زیر فرمان و رای تو 'under your command and counsel' (زیر = 'under/subject to', فرمان = 'command', رای = 'counsel/plan'), finished by باد 'may'. The structure expresses a wish that the whole world be subject to the addressee's rule and counsel.
segments.s549-g33: به جایی رسیدی هم‌اندر سخن is a prepositional + verbal phrase: به جایی (‘to/such a place/point’) is an adverbial phrase of result, رسیدی is 2nd-person singular past (perfect) of رسیدن ‘to reach/arrive’ with the subject implied (تو), and هم‌اندر سخن (‘in the same/that speech’) is an archaic adverbial phrase modifying the verb. Word order is typical Persian: adverbial phrase + verb, with the topical/poetic modifier هم‌اندر placed before the noun سخن.
segments.s549-g34: که (relative 'that') introduces the result/relative clause. نو شد is the past tense of شدن ‘to become’ (شد = became) with نو ‘new’ as the predicative adjective. ز رای تو is an ablative/agent-like phrase ‘from/by your counsel’ (ز = from/because of; رای = counsel/opinion), and مرد کهن is the affected noun phrase ‘the old man’ (مرد = man/person, کهن = old/ancient). The clause literally says ‘that the old man became new by your counsel.’
segments.s549-g35: خردها (literally ‘wisdoms’; plural form) is the grammatical subject. فزون شد is a compound predicate meaning ‘became abundant/increased’ (فزون = abundant/increasing, شد = became). ز گفتار تو is a causal/ablative phrase ‘because of/through your speech’ (ز = from/because of, گفتار = speech, تو = your). The verb is 3rd-person singular despite خردها being plural — a typical poetic treatment where a collective plural takes a singular verb.
segments.s549-g36: جهان is the subject ‘the world’. گشت is the past of گشتن here meaning ‘became/turned’ and روشن is the predicative adjective ‘bright/clear/enlightened’. به دیدار تو is a prepositional phrase literally ‘at/by the sight/meeting of you’ (به = toward/at/by, دیدار = sight/appearance/meeting, تو = you), meaning ‘at your sight/appearance’ or ‘when you are seen’. The structure is Subject + Verb + Predicate + adverbial.
segments.s549-g37: بدین انجمن is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘in/at this assembly’ (بدین = archaic/formal variant of به + این ‘in this’, انجمن = assembly). هرک (‘هر که’) is the indefinite relative ‘whoever’. دارد is the present/past 3rd-person singular of داشتن ‘to have’ and here means ‘has’. نژاد is the direct object/topic ‘lineage/stock’. The phrase orders the location first, then the indefinite subject and possession (‘whoever has lineage’).
segments.s549-g38: به تو شادمانند is a predication: به تو (‘toward you/about you’) + شادمانند, an archaic/poetic 3rd-person plural form meaning ‘are joyful/rejoice’. The following وز داد شاد is coordinated: و + ز (here written together as وز) functions as ‘and from’ (و = and, ز = from/because of) and داد شاد means ‘(they are) happy because of justice’ where داد is the noun ‘justice’ and شاد ‘happy’. The clause implies parallel predicates (‘they rejoice in you and are happy from justice’).
segments.s549-g39: توی خلعت ایزدی is a locative/locutional phrase: توی (an emphatic/poetic form of توی/توئی) = ‘in/among’, خلعت ایزدی = ‘divine robe of honor’ (خلعت = robe of investiture/robe of honour, ایزدی = divine). بخت را places بخت (‘Fortune’ or ‘Luck’) in topical/object position with the object/topic marker را, preparing for the verbal or nominal predicate that follows (i.e., Fortune is what the following actions concern).
segments.s549-g40: کلاه و کمر بستن و تخت را lists the items and the (implied) action: کلاه و کمر ‘hat/cap and girdle’ followed by بستن ‘to bind/fasten’ used here in a poetry-typical non-finite or telegraphic way (the finite verb is implied or understood as past: ‘(was) bound’). The final و تخت را adds ‘and the throne’ (تخت marked with را). In effect the verse enumerates things Fortune received or had bound on it — poetic ellipsis leaves the main finite verb understood from context.
segments.s549-g9: به جایی رسیدی is a verbal phrase meaning “you reached/you came to a place.” به + noun marks direction/target (here “to/into a place”), جایی is an indefinite noun “a place/somewhere,” and رسیدی is 2nd-person singular past (you arrived/reached). که begins a relative/subordinate clause (“that/where”). مرغ و دده are coordinated nouns (“bird(s) and elders/old men”) serving as the subject of the following verb.
segments.s549-g10: زنند is the 3rd-person plural verbal form of زدن (here “to beat/strike; to play/perform”), i.e. “they beat/they perform.” The token list split the verb into زن + ند; ند here functions as the 3pl verbal ending (so it is not the separate lexical item listed). از پس و پیش is a fixed adverbial phrase “from behind and before / on both sides,” with از marking origin/position and پس = “after/behind,” پیش = “before/in front of.” تختت is تخت + enclitic -ت “your throne.” رده is a noun “row/rank/series” and here names the formation in which those performers are arranged (“in ranks”).
segments.s549-g11: بزرگ جهان is a nominal phrase meaning “great one of the world” (literal order: adjective + noun). از کران تا کران is a standard correlative span meaning “from edge to edge / from horizon to horizon” (از ... تا marks the limits). The phrase as a whole qualifies the subject praised in the surrounding lines.
segments.s549-g12: سرافراز is an adjective “proud/lofty/held high.” بر is the preposition “over/above” indicating superiority. تاجور مهتران is a genitive/compound phrase naming the class of people over whom one is lofty; in this manuscript excerpt the listed dictionary senses for تاجور and مهتران do not match the classical sense intended here (a group of chiefs/nobles), so treat those two words as denoting leading men/chiefs in context.
segments.s549-g13: که introduces a relative/interrogative sense here (“who/that”). داند is 3rd-person singular present of دانستن “to know,” used idiomatically as “who can/knows how.” صفت کردن is a light-verb construction: صفت (an attribute/description) + کردن (to make/do) = “to describe/compose a description.” از داد تو: از (from/of) takes داد “justice” as source and تو is the 2nd-person possessive “your.” Together: “who can describe (the) qualities that come from your justice.”
segments.s549-g14: که again links/explains the previous clause (“for/that”); داد و بزرگیست is a nominal assertion meaning “justice and greatness are ...” Note بزرگیست is the fused form of بزرگی + است (“is/are”), here asserting the quality “greatness.” بنیاد تو places the predicate as “the foundation of you/your rule.” Overall: “for justice and greatness are your foundation.”
segments.s549-g15: همان is a demonstrative 'that/same' modifying آفرین (here a noun 'praise' or 'acclamation'), placed before the noun as in Persian. در فزایش is a prepositional/phrase 'in increase' meaning 'by increasing' or 'to increase', and کنیم is the present-subjunctive/jussive 1st-person-plural of کردن ('let us ...'), so the clause literally says 'let us make/increase that praise.'
segments.s549-g16: خدای جهان is a genitive phrase 'God of the world' (خدای = God + جهان = world). را is the definite direct-object marker attaching to that noun, and نیایش کنیم is the noun نیایش 'prayer/worship' plus کنیم (same 1st-plural subjunctive of کردن), so this clause means 'let us worship/offer prayer to the God of the world.' The verb ending -یم supplies the omitted subject 'we.'
segments.s549-g1: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. The prepositional phrase بر تخت 'on the throne' precedes the verb بنشست (past of نشستن, 'sat down'), and the subject شاه اردشیر follows the verb — a common word order in classical Persian where the verb can come before the subject. بنشست is simple past, literal 'sat down'.
segments.s549-g2: بشد is an impersonal/past sense 'it happened / there came' (used here to mean 'appeared'). پیش + گاهش functions as 'before his seat/his presence' with the possessive suffix -ش on گاه (here 'his [place/time/presence]'). یکی مرد پیر is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain elderly man' (یکی = 'a certain', مرد = 'man', پیر = 'old'), adjective following the noun as typical in Persian.
segments.s549-g3: نام is the (grammatical) subject 'the name'; آن پیر is the noun phrase 'that elder' with the demonstrative آن. خراد is the predicative nominative ('was Khurad') and بود is the copula in the past. The initial کجا is an archaic/exclamatory particle placing or introducing the statement (classical style) rather than a literal 'where'.
segments.s549-g4: زبان و روانش is a coordinated subject 'his tongue (speech) and his soul' (روان + possessive -ش). پر + از + داد is a common pattern: پر 'full' followed by از + noun to indicate content 'full of justice' (داد). بود is the past copula 'was'. Note the figurative use of زبان = 'speech/utterance'.
segments.s549-g5: چنین داد پاسخ is a verb-object phrase 'gave such an answer' (چنین = 'thus/such', داد = past 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer/response'). The following که‌ای شهریار is a vocative: که‌ای (an archaic/emphatic particle + ای) = 'O', شهریار = 'king/sovereign', addressing the ruler directly.
segments.s549-g6: این جمله is a compact optative/wish clause. انوشه here functions as 'everlasting/immortal' (classical/onomastic adjective), بدی is the abstract predicate (rendered here as 'good/fortune' in context), and تا + بود + روزگار forms a temporal clause 'as long as time/world lasts' with بود as the copula at the end. The whole segment expresses a wish: 'May everlasting good be yours while time exists.'
segments.s549-g7: همیشه is the adverb 'always' modifying the phrase; بوی 'scent/fragrance' is used metaphorically for 'air/omen' or 'atmosphere'. شاد و پیروزبخت are coordinated adjectives 'joyful and fortunate/prosperous' describing the quality being wished. In poetry this kind of nominal phrase conveys an ongoing blessing.
segments.s549-g8: به تو marks the beneficiary 'to/for you'. شادمان is the predicate/adjective 'rejoicing/happy'. کشور و تاج و تخت is a coordinated list 'realm and crown and throne' serving as the entities that are wished to be joyful for the king; word order places the beneficiary before the predicate and the things affected after it, a flexible arrangement common in Persian poetic diction.
segments.s549-g25: پراگنده شد is a classical/perfective construction meaning “became scattered/widespread” (پراگنده = 'widespread', شد = 'became'). The noun list غارت و جنگ و موش follows as the theme that has become widespread; Persian lists can end with a bare noun without a linking particle. The sequence keeps the verb (شد) before the full enumerated noun phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s549-g26: نیاید همی is a negative present-subjunctive/gnomic form meaning “does not come / does not reach” with همی (classical particle) adding a continuous/emphatic sense (‘still/continually’ or an emphatic negation). جوش دشمن is a genitive-like phrase ‘the enemy’s surge/commotion’; به گوش is a dative phrase ‘to the ear’. Word order is verb (نیاید) + modifier (همی) + subject/phrase, typical of older Persian poetry.
segments.s549-g27: بماناد is an archaic optative/subjunctive form meaning ‘may (he) remain’ or ‘let (him) remain’. این شاه is the noun phrase ‘this king’; تا جاودان is a temporal/goal phrase ‘until/so long as eternal’ — together it expresses the wish that the king’s rule endure forever.
segments.s549-g28: همیشه is an adverb ‘always’. سر و کار is an idiomatic compound meaning ‘affairs; dealings’ (سر and کار together create this sense), followed by با موبدان ‘with the mōbeds/priests’. The verbless clause is common in wishes: the verb from the previous clause (بماناد) carries over, so this segment completes the wished condition for the king.
segments.s549-g29: نه کس is a negated indefinite meaning ‘no one’. چون تو is a simile/adverbial phrase ‘like you’. دارد is the present indicative ‘has/possesses’. ز شاهان is a poetic genitive/partitive ‘of/from the kings’ and خرد is the noun ‘wisdom’. Word order here is negator + subject + comparative phrase + verb + genitive + head noun — typical of Persian poetic inversion.
segments.s549-g30: نه begins another negated clause. اندیشه ‘thought’ is the subject, از رای تو is a prepositional/agent-like phrase ‘from your counsel/opinion’ (ز/از marking source or domain), and بگذرد here means ‘pass beyond / exceed’ (i.e., ‘no thought goes beyond your counsel’). The verb is present-subjunctive/indicative depending on context but functions as a general statement about deference to the addressee’s opinion.
segments.s549-g31: پیی is an archaic/prepositional item meaning ‘after’ or ‘in pursuit of’ and here introduces a temporal/sequential nuance. برفگندی is an adjective/noun ‘snow‑white (one)’; به ایران is the directional phrase ‘to Iran’; ز داد uses the poetic preposition ز (from/by) with داد ‘justice’ — together: ‘afterward a snow‑white (one) came to Iran by/from justice’ (phrasing is compressed as often in classical verse).
segments.s549-g32: که is the subordinating conjunction ‘that’ (introducing purpose/result). فرزند ما ‘our child/descendant’ is the subject; باشد is a subjunctive/optative form ‘may be’ (expressing a wish or potential state); از داد شاد is a short phrase ‘happy because of/by justice’ (از marking cause/reason). Overall this is a purposive/result clause: ‘so that our child may be glad through justice’.

sentences.s549-s9: That as long as we live in your time we are with you, and in every good deed our thought is of you.
sentences.s549-s10: We would buy the sight of your face — likewise your courteous speech and your affection.
sentences.s549-s11: May you be safe, for by you we are secure; may it not be that we break your covenant.
sentences.s549-s12: You have closed the road to our ill‑fated ones from India and from China, and our bearers.
sentences.s549-s21: May this king remain with love and justice; the world has no Khosrow like you in its memory.
sentences.s549-s22: The world is entirely from your counsel and royal glory; refreshing is he who dwells full in your shade.
sentences.s549-s23: May your place always be at the head of the throne; may the world be under your command and counsel.
sentences.s549-s17: You reached such a point in speech that the old man became new by your counsel.
sentences.s549-s18: Wisdom increased because of your speech; the world became bright at your sight.
sentences.s549-s19: In this assembly whoever has lineage rejoices in you and is glad because of justice.
sentences.s549-s20: In the divine robe of honor, Fortune (was) given a cap and girdle bound, and (given) a throne.
sentences.s549-s5: You have come to a place where birds and elders beat (or perform) in ranks before and behind your throne.
sentences.s549-s6: Great in the world, from edge to edge, lofty above the chief nobles.
sentences.s549-s7: Who can set down a description of your attributes derived from your justice? For justice and greatness are your foundation.
sentences.s549-s8: Let us increase that same praise; let us worship the God of the world.
sentences.s549-s1: When King Ardashir sat upon the throne, a certain old man appeared before his seat.
sentences.s549-s2: The name of that old man was Khurad.
sentences.s549-s3: He gave this reply: 'O sovereign, may everlasting good be yours as long as time endures.'
sentences.s549-s4: May the fragrance of constant joy and prosperous fortune always bring happiness to you, to your realm, crown, and throne.
sentences.s549-s13: Plunder, war, and mice were scattered far and wide; the enemy's tumult did not reach the ear.
sentences.s549-s14: May this king remain forever; may his affairs always be with the mōbeds (Zoroastrian priests).
sentences.s549-s15: No one among kings has wisdom like you; no thought goes beyond your counsel.
sentences.s549-s16: Afterward a snow‑white one came into Iran by justice, that our child might be glad because of justice.
